title: Infrastructure as Code
summary: Terraform fundamentals, state management, modules, Helm charts for Kubernetes, and IaC best practices.

sections.content:
## What is Infrastructure as Code?

IaC means managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable configuration files instead of manual processes or interactive GUIs. Infrastructure becomes versioned, repeatable, and auditable.

**Key tools:**
- **Terraform** — cloud-agnostic, declarative, HCL language
- **Pulumi** — IaC with real programming languages (Java, Python, TypeScript)
- **AWS CDK** — AWS-specific, real programming languages
- **Helm** — Kubernetes package manager, templates

sections.title: Terraform Workflow
sections.content:
graph LR
    Code[Write .tf files] -->|terraform init| Init[Download providers\n& modules]
    Init -->|terraform plan| Plan[Show what will change]
    Plan -->|review| Review{Looks good?}
    Review -->|yes| Apply[terraform apply]
    Review -->|no| Code
    Apply --> State[Update state file\nterraform.tfstate]
    State --> Infra[Real Infrastructure\nAWS/GCP/Azure]
    style Apply fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style Plan fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## Terraform State Management

Terraform tracks real infrastructure in a **state file** (`terraform.tfstate`). This file maps your config to real resources.

**Critical rules:**
1. **Never store state in Git** — contains sensitive data (passwords, IPs)
2. **Use remote state** — S3 + DynamoDB for locking, Terraform Cloud
3. **One state per environment** — separate state for dev/staging/prod
4. **State locking** — prevent concurrent applies corrupting state

```hcl
# Remote state with locking
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-tf-state"
    key            = "${var.environment}/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"  # locking
  }
}

# Reference state from another config
data "terraform_remote_state" "networking" {
  backend = "s3"
  config = {
    bucket = "my-tf-state"
    key    = "networking/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

# Use output from another config
subnet_ids = data.terraform_remote_state.networking.outputs.private_subnets
```

## Helm Charts for Kubernetes

Helm is the Kubernetes package manager. A **Chart** is a templated collection of Kubernetes manifests.

```
my-service/              ← Chart directory
  Chart.yaml             ← Chart metadata
  values.yaml            ← Default values
  values-prod.yaml       ← Production overrides
  templates/
    deployment.yaml      ← Templated Deployment
    service.yaml         ← Templated Service
    ingress.yaml         ← Templated Ingress
    _helpers.tpl         ← Template helpers
```

```yaml
# values.yaml
replicaCount: 2
image:
  repository: registry/order-service
  tag: latest
resources:
  requests:
    memory: 256Mi
    cpu: 250m

# templates/deployment.yaml
spec:
  replicas: {{ .Values.replicaCount }}
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - image: {{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}
```

```bash
# Deploy with Helm
helm upgrade --install order-service ./charts/order-service \
  --namespace production \
  --values values-prod.yaml \
  --set image.tag=$GIT_SHA

# Rollback
helm rollback order-service 1  # roll back to revision 1
```

sections.title: IaC Layered Architecture
sections.content:
graph TB
    L1[Layer 1: Cloud Foundation\nTerraform: VPC, subnets, IAM, DNS]
    L2[Layer 2: Platform\nTerraform: EKS cluster, RDS, Redis]
    L3[Layer 3: Applications\nHelm: Deploy services to K8s]
    L4[Layer 4: Config\nConfigMaps, Secrets, feature flags]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4
    style L1 fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style L2 fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style L3 fill:#2563eb,color:#fff
    style L4 fill:#059669,color:#fff

sections.question: Why should Terraform state never be stored in a Git repository?
options: Git cannot handle binary files like the state file
The state file contains sensitive values (passwords, connection strings) and Git history makes them permanently visible even after deletion
Terraform requires state to be stored in S3 specifically
Git conflicts with state files cause infrastructure corruption
sections.explanation: Terraform state often contains sensitive data like database passwords, private keys, and connection strings. Committing to Git exposes these permanently in history — even after deletion, `git log` reveals them. Additionally, concurrent applies without locking can corrupt state. Use remote state (S3 + DynamoDB) which supports encryption and locking.
